codeSnapshot.label: backpropagation.py
codeSnapshot.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

# PyTorch autograd implements backprop via dynamic computation graph
model = nn.Sequential(
    nn.Linear(4, 3),
    nn.ReLU(),
    nn.Linear(3, 2),
    nn.ReLU(),
    nn.Linear(2, 1)
)

x = torch.randn(5, 4, requires_grad=False)
y = torch.randn(5, 1)

# Forward pass: build computation graph
loss = nn.MSELoss()(model(x), y)

# Backward pass: compute ∂loss/∂w for all w in model
loss.backward()

# Access gradients
for name, param in model.named_parameters():
    print(f"{name}: grad shape {param.grad.shape}, norm {param.grad.norm():.4f}")

# Manual backprop for a 2-layer network
# Forward:
# z1 = W1*x + b1
# a1 = relu(z1)
# z2 = W2*a1 + b2
# loss = (z2 - y)^2 / n

# Backward (chain rule):
# dL/dz2 = 2*(z2-y)/n
# dL/dW2 = dL/dz2 * a1^T
# dL/db2 = sum(dL/dz2)
# dL/da1 = W2^T * dL/dz2
# dL/dz1 = dL/da1 * relu'(z1)  [relu'(z)=1 if z>0, else 0]
# dL/dW1 = dL/dz1 * x^T
# dL/db1 = sum(dL/dz1)

W1 = torch.randn(3, 4, requires_grad=True)
b1 = torch.zeros(3, requires_grad=True)
W2 = torch.randn(1, 3, requires_grad=True)
b2 = torch.zeros(1, requires_grad=True)

z1 = x @ W1.T + b1
a1 = torch.relu(z1)
z2 = a1 @ W2.T + b2
loss = ((z2 - y)**2).mean()
loss.backward()  # PyTorch does the chain rule for us

eli3: Backpropagation is the algorithm that tells each weight in a neural network 'how much did you contribute to the error, and in which direction should you change?' It works by applying the chain rule of calculus backwards through the network: starting from the output error and propagating it back through each layer.

Imagine you're the coach of a relay race team that just lost. Backprop is figuring out exactly how much each runner contributed to the loss: the last runner's time, then the second-to-last's contribution to the last's performance, and so on back to the first runner. Everyone gets blamed (or credited) proportionally.

The key insight is efficiency: computing all gradients from scratch would be O(W·N) where W is the number of weights. Backprop reuses intermediate computations (the chain rule's gradient products) to compute all gradients in O(W) — the same cost as a single forward pass.

whenWhy.title: Training every neural network that exists
whenWhy.body: Backpropagation (Rumelhart, Hinton & Williams, 1986) is how all gradient-based neural network training works. Frameworks like PyTorch and JAX implement it automatically via automatic differentiation (autograd). Every call to loss.backward() runs a reverse-mode automatic differentiation pass that is mathematically equivalent to backpropagation.

whenWhy.title: Understanding gradient flow for architecture design
whenWhy.body: Understanding backprop is essential for diagnosing training issues: vanishing gradients (gradients become too small in early layers), exploding gradients (too large), and dead neurons (ReLU units that never receive nonzero gradient). Skip connections in ResNets, LSTM gating mechanisms, and gradient clipping all exist to address backprop failure modes.

whenWhy.title: Custom loss functions and gradient computation
whenWhy.body: When designing custom losses or architectures, you need to verify that gradients flow correctly. PyTorch's autograd.gradcheck function numerically verifies gradients against finite differences — essential for custom CUDA kernels, sparse operations, or non-standard activation functions that bypass autograd.

deepDiveIntro: Backpropagation computes how much each weight contributed to the error by applying the chain rule of calculus backwards through the network. The key efficiency is that intermediate error signals are computed once per layer and reused — so the total cost is about twice the forward pass, not multiplied by the number of weights. [1]

Frameworks like PyTorch implement this as reverse-mode automatic differentiation: they build a computation graph during the forward pass, then traverse it backwards in topological order to compute gradients. This is exactly what happens when you call loss.backward(). [2]

deepDive.heading: The Chain Rule in Matrix Form
deepDive.body: Each layer produces an error signal by taking the next layer's error signal, passing it backwards through the weight matrix, then multiplying element-wise by the derivative of the activation function. This error signal tells you how much each pre-activation value contributed to the final loss. The gradient for the weight matrix is then just an outer product of the error signal and the layer's input from the forward pass. [1]

deepDive.heading: Computational Cost
deepDive.body: The backward pass costs about twice the forward pass in compute. The bigger problem is memory: to compute gradients for each layer's weights, backpropagation needs to know what the activations were during the forward pass — so all intermediate layer outputs must be stored. For large transformers, this activation memory often exceeds GPU RAM. Gradient checkpointing solves this by recomputing some activations during the backward pass instead of storing them, trading extra compute for lower peak memory. [1]

deepDive.heading: Forward vs Reverse Mode Autodiff
deepDive.body: Forward-mode autodiff computes how one input affects all outputs — useful when there are far fewer inputs than outputs. Reverse-mode autodiff (backpropagation) computes how all inputs affect one scalar output, which is exactly what you need in machine learning: many weights, one loss value. That's why PyTorch uses reverse mode — you only need one backward pass to get gradients for every weight in the network, no matter how many millions there are. [2]

takeaways: δₗ = (Wₗ₊₁)ᵀδₗ₊₁ ⊙ f'(zₗ) — recursive error signal; ∂L/∂Wₗ = δₗaₗ₋₁ᵀ. [1]
Cost: 2× forward pass in compute, O(Σnₗ) in memory (all activations must be stored). [1]
Reverse-mode autodiff: O(1) backward passes for any number of parameters. [2]
Gradient checkpointing: recompute activations during backward to save memory (3× compute, O(√L) memory). [1]
PyTorch autograd builds a dynamic computation graph; loss.backward() runs reverse-mode autodiff. [2]

edgeCases.q: Why is backprop memory expensive and how does gradient checkpointing help?
edgeCases.a: During the backward pass, the gradient ∂L/∂Wₗ = δₗ·aₗ₋₁ᵀ requires the activations aₗ₋₁ from the forward pass. A naive implementation stores all activations throughout the forward pass — O(L·B·n) memory for L layers, batch size B, hidden size n. For a 100-layer transformer with B=32, n=4096, this is ~50GB. Gradient checkpointing (Chen et al., 2016) only stores activations at a subset of 'checkpoint' layers and recomputes the rest during backprop. With √L checkpoints, memory reduces from O(L) to O(√L) at the cost of one additional forward pass (3× compute total instead of 2×).

edgeCases.q: What is the vanishing gradient problem in the context of backpropagation?
edgeCases.a: In the backward pass, δₗ = (Wₗ₊₁)ᵀδₗ₊₁ ⊙ f'(zₗ). The gradient from layer L back to layer l is a product of L−l matrices and activation derivatives: ∂L/∂aₗ = ΠₖWₖᵀ · (Π f'(zₖ)) · ∂L/∂aL. For sigmoid activations: f'(z) = σ(z)(1−σ(z)) ∈ (0, 0.25]. In a 100-layer network: gradient ∝ 0.25^100 ≈ 10^{-60} — numerically zero. Early layers learn nothing. Solutions: ReLU (f'=1 for z>0), skip connections (additive paths bypass multiplication), LSTM gates, layer/batch normalisation.

sources.label: Rumelhart, Hinton & Williams (1986) — Learning representations by back-propagating errors, Nature 323:533–536
Goodfellow, Bengio & Courville — Deep Learning, Ch. 6 (Deep Feedforward Networks)
